extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system is a lab‑scale gantry crane inspired by the harbor cranes of the Port of Antwerp. It is a small, tabletop‑size crane that transports lightweight pucks representing containers between predefined locations. The physical twin comprises the crane mechanics (frame, hoist and trolley), a stepper hoist motor driven by a TMC4671‑Eval motor controller, and low‑level controllers. Interaction with the hardware is provided through a Python‑based Physical Crane Interface that connects to the motor controller, a Control Application (Python) that issues motion commands, a Logging Application (Python) that continuously records the crane’s state, and a Human Machine Interface (Python front‑end) for operator control and monitoring. The setup operates in a logistics/ container‑handling domain and is deliberately simple and open‑source to serve as a reproducible digital‑twin exemplar.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin supplies several distinct services: (1) a Trajectory Generator Service that uses a kinematic model of the crane to compute optimal movement trajectories with zero residual swing; (2) a Simulation Service that emulates the crane and its controllers and can run the generated trajectories for what‑if analysis; (3) a Continuous Validation Service that continuously compares measured trajectory data against the simulation, computes distance metrics, checks them against a configurable threshold and raises alerts via the dashboard; (4) historical data logging performed by the Logging Application into a TimescaleDB database; (5) real‑time data visualization through a Grafana dashboard; (6) control support via the Control Application; and (7) inter‑service coordination via an MQTT broker. These services target both operators (monitoring, alerting, visualization) and researchers (simulation, validation, optimization).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The twin is implemented with open‑source tooling: Python is used for the Physical Crane Interface, Control, Logging, and HMI applications; TimescaleDB (a PostgreSQL time‑series extension) stores all measured and simulated data; Grafana provides the web‑based dashboard; an MQTT broker (standard MQTT protocol) handles signalling between services; all components are packaged as Docker containers and launched with Docker‑Compose as illustrated in the deployment diagram. The Trajectory Generator and Simulation services rely on a kinematic model derived from CAD data (not explicitly named). Architecture documentation follows the C4 model. The entire source code and configuration are publicly hosted on GitHub, enabling reproducible setup.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The exemplar implements a single digital twin instance that is structurally decomposed into six logical containers: a TimescaleDB database, a Grafana dashboard, a Trajectory Generator Service (kinematics model), a Simulation Service, a Continuous Validation Service, and an MQTT broker for inter‑service signaling. These containers together realize the digital side, while the physical side consists of the Physical Crane, its controller, and a Python interface. No hierarchy of multiple twin instances or decentralized twin network is described; responsibilities are partitioned per container.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a container‑based microservice architecture orchestrated with Docker‑compose. Core services (Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation) communicate via an MQTT broker (publish‑subscribe) and share state through a TimescaleDB time‑series database. Results are visualized on a Grafana dashboard. Python applications implement the Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, and Human‑Machine Interface. This pattern provides a service‑oriented architecture with a central messaging bus and a shared data store for coordination and resource management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external components is realized through standard tools and protocols: MQTT is used as the lightweight publish‑subscribe protocol for inter‑service communication; TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL extension) stores time‑series data; Grafana provides data visualization; Docker‑compose manages container deployment. No explicit coupling to external enterprise systems or other digital twins is documented.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The digital twin comprises a kinematic model of the gantry crane used by the Trajectory Generator Service to compute optimal movement trajectories (ensuring zero residual swing), a simulation model implemented by the Simulation Service that reproduces the crane’s dynamics and controller behavior, a TimescaleDB database that persistently stores all logged state data, a Grafana dashboard that visualizes the stored data, and an MQTT broker that provides lightweight signaling between services; data flow proceeds from the Physical Crane Interface into the Logging Application, which writes time‑stamped state records to TimescaleDB, while the Continuous Validation Service consumes both the logged measurements and simulated trajectory outputs to assess model conformity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The actuator suite consists of a stepper motor driving the hoist mechanism, controlled by a TMC4671‑Eval motor controller; the controller is accessed via a Python‑based Physical Crane Interface that exposes commands to the Control Application, enabling remote start/stop, speed, and position commands, with actuation limits implicitly defined by the motor specifications and controller firmware. The system also includes the overall crane hardware (rails, trolley, and winch) that is driven by the same controller chain, but explicit range or safety constraints are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured through a Continuous Validation Service that continuously compares measured trajectory data from the physical crane with the simulated trajectories generated by the Simulation Service, computing distance metrics and checking them against a predefined acceptance threshold; violations trigger operator alerts via the Grafana dashboard. The trajectory generator’s requirement for zero residual swing defines a high‑fidelity kinematic constraint, and the validation process constitutes the primary verification method described for the twin’s analytical and behavioral models.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensor data (swing‑angle encoder, anemometer, motor state feedback) is collected by an Arduino board. The Arduino is accessed by the Physical Crane Interface – a Python program that connects to the crane controllers (likely via a serial/USB link). A Logging Application continuously reads the crane’s state through this interface and writes the raw time‑series measurements into a TimescaleDB instance using the PostgreSQL wire protocol. Measurements are also published on MQTT topics via the Eclipse Mosquitto broker so that other twin services (e.g., Continuous Validation Service) can subscribe. The documents state that logging is continuous but give no explicit sampling frequency, preprocessing steps, or quality‑assurance mechanisms; therefore the data are stored as‑is and later integrated by services that consume the MQTT stream or query the database.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The digital twin generates control commands through several services. The Trajectory Generator Service computes optimal movement trajectories (kinematic model, swing‑free) and forwards them to the Control Application. The Control Application uses the same Physical Crane Interface to transmit set‑point commands to the Arduino‑controlled motor controllers (cart and hoist) – presumably as serial command messages. Operators can also issue commands via the Human‑Machine Interface, which routes them through the Control Application. The Continuous Validation Service monitors execution and, when a threshold is breached, notifies the operator via the Grafana dashboard. No explicit command format, authentication, or confirmation protocol is described in the documents; the interaction assumes trusted local execution on the same host.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All twin services are deployed as Docker containers on a single personal computer, forming a local Docker network. Communication among services uses: • MQTT (Eclipse Mosquitto) over TCP for signalling and data streaming; • PostgreSQL protocol for TimescaleDB access; • HTTP/HTTPS (Grafana dashboard) to query the database. The documents do not detail network topology beyond the single‑host Docker setup, nor do they specify bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security mechanisms such as TLS or authentication for MQTT or database connections.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The exemplar runs on‑premise on a personal computer. Core components (TimescaleDB, Grafana, Eclipse Mosquitto) are containerised with Docker and orchestrated via a Docker‑Compose file that handles first‑time initialization. The Python‑based control, logging, and service applications are installed from the pip‑distributed gantrylib module and run directly on the host OS. Storage requirements are driven by TimescaleDB for time‑series logs; compute requirements are modest (a typical workstation can host all containers). Scalability is limited to adding more services or scaling containers manually; no cloud or auto‑scaling features are mentioned. Availability relies on Docker’s container lifecycle management, but explicit redundancy or high‑availability strategies are not described.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The lab‑scale gantry crane digital twin is developed as an open‑source project hosted on GitHub. Its software stack consists of multiple Docker containers orchestrated with a single Docker‑compose command. The containers implement the Database (TimescaleDB), Dashboard (Grafana), Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, and an MQTT broker for inter‑service signalling. Supporting components—Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, and Human‑Machine Interface—are Python programs that communicate with the containers. The overall architecture is documented with the C4 modelling notation. Quality assurance is exercised through continuous validation methods that compare live measurements with simulated trajectories, and model‑validation techniques are reused across experiments. Evolution of the twin is demonstrated by applying the DarTwin notation in later research, indicating that new services or updated models are incorporated by extending the container configuration and rebuilding the Docker images.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin generates decision‑support insights through several specialised services: the Trajectory Generator Service computes optimal, swing‑free movement trajectories for the crane; the Simulation Service predicts the crane’s behaviour for those trajectories; and the Continuous Validation Service continuously compares executed trajectories with their simulated counterparts, computes distance metrics and triggers alerts when a predefined threshold is breached. All data are stored in the TimescaleDB database and visualised on a Grafana dashboard that shows real‑time trajectory traces with confidence bounds (simulated vs. measured). Operators interact with the system via a Python‑based Human‑Machine Interface that allows configuration, manual control, monitoring of status, and acknowledgment of validation alerts.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be

for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

In it are controls for the crane’s movement, homing and zeroing, the real-time status and configurations of the logger w.r.t database writeout. Figure 7 shows that with the single docker compose command the containers and the associated network are up and running. Lastly, fig. 8 shows some traces on the dashboard. Those traces have also been simulated by the simulator, as indicated by the upper (in blue) and lower (in yellow) confidence bounds derived from the simulation. Fig. 8. Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces. _A. Usage in Publications_ The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying poster used the crane as illustrative example. Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended the continuous validation method of [5] with classification techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar. _B. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to an entire harbor system, with a container identification system to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will be revisited in the second semester.

I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

an affordable and straightforward example. The exemplar is
hosted on GitHub [2] and contains documentation on how to get

encapsulate the examples and is built on the open ARCHES
Digital Twin Framework which is built on top of the ROS

The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and

_A. Architecture_

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give



an overview of the components powering it. We should note
that for this physical system, we take a liberal interpretation
of what goes in a C4 Model component diagram.
Figure 4 shows this component diagram, again for brevity
and readability only the most necessary relationships are
shown. It contains the **Crane Frame** consisting of all the
hardware constituting the physical structure of the crane.
Two **Motors** and associated **Controllers**, one to control the
cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement. An
**Electromagnet** at the end of the hoist to pick up containers,
a **Swing Angle Encoder** measures the swinging motion of
the hoist, and an **Anemometer** measures the wind speed. An
**Arduino** measures and controls those last three components.
Lastly a **PSU** powers the electronics of the crane.


_B. Implementation_


Regarding the implementation, we wanted to keep the
operation of the system as simple as possible, that is, plug
in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of
the software on that computer. Figure 5 shows the distribution
of the C4 Containers visually.

Containers in a Python module named _gantrylib_ . To use
this module, it suffices to install it with _pip_ . This way it
remains easy for researchers to implement additional digital
twin services.

**Dashboard** C4 Containers are containerized with Docker. A

docker compose file is provided to bring the containers up and
down with a single command. For the first time setup, all of
the database and dashboard initialization are also automatically
handled by this docker compose file.

choice for Grafana was made because of (i) it’s wide support
for data sources and (ii) again the fact that it’s open source.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

is another field for which well described exemplars can be

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.

in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.
